name: Hyperlipidemia Management Protocol (ACC/AHA 2018 Guidelines)
description: This protocol outlines an evidence-based approach to the diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management of hyperlipidemia in primary care, adhering to the ACC/AHA 2018 Cholesterol Guidelines. It focuses on reducing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk through lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy.

templateData.duration: Initial phase: 3-6 months for lifestyle and medication titration. Ongoing: Lifelong management.
goals: Achieve a ≥50% reduction in LDL-C for patients with clinical ASCVD or primary LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients with severe primary hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) who cannot tolerate high-intensity statins.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients aged 40-75 with diabetes and LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients aged 40-75 with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL and a 10-year ASCVD risk of 7.5% to 19.9%.
Maintain non-HDL-C levels below target thresholds based on individual risk assessment.
Improve overall cardiovascular health and reduce the risk of future ASCVD events.

items: Perform 10-year ASCVD risk assessment using the Pooled Cohort Equations for patients aged 40-75 without ASCVD or diabetes.
Educate patient on the importance of hyperlipidemia management and its role in ASCVD prevention.
Discuss shared decision-making regarding statin therapy initiation, considering patient preferences and potential side effects.
Provide materials on heart-healthy diet and physical activity.
Initiate appropriate intensity statin therapy based on patient's ASCVD risk category and LDL-C levels.
Titrate statin dose as needed to achieve target LDL-C reduction, monitoring for side effects.
Consider adding non-statin therapies (e.g., ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for very high-risk patients who do not achieve sufficient LDL-C reduction on maximally tolerated statin therapy.
Monitor lipid panel (LDL-C, HDL-C, Triglycerides, Non-HDL-C) 4-12 weeks after statin initiation or dose change, then annually or as clinically indicated.
Monitor liver function tests (ALT) and creatine kinase (CK) before statin initiation and as clinically indicated for symptoms.
Assess patient adherence to medication and lifestyle recommendations at each visit.
Address any barriers to adherence, including cost, side effects, and understanding of treatment plan.
Evaluate for secondary causes of hyperlipidemia (e.g., hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, obstructive liver disease, certain medications).
Consider specific guidelines for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia.
Manage hypertriglyceridemia, especially severe cases (>500 mg/dL), to prevent pancreatitis.

medications.name: Atorvastatin (Lipitor)
medications.instructions: High-intensity (40-80 mg) for patients requiring ≥50% LDL-C reduction. Moderate-intensity (10-20 mg) for patients requiring 30-49% LDL-C reduction or unable to tolerate high-intensity.

medications.name: Rosuvastatin (Crestor)
medications.instructions: High-intensity (20-40 mg) for patients requiring ≥50% LDL-C reduction. Moderate-intensity (5-10 mg) for patients requiring 30-49% LDL-C reduction or unable to tolerate high-intensity.

medications.name: Simvastatin (Zocor)
medications.instructions: Moderate-intensity (20-40 mg). Note: 80 mg dose is not recommended due to increased risk of myopathy, especially with certain drug interactions.

medications.name: Ezetimibe (Zetia)
medications.instructions: Add-on therapy for patients who do not achieve sufficient LDL-C reduction on maximally tolerated statin therapy, especially in very high-risk individuals.

lifestyle: Adopt a heart-healthy diet (e.g., Mediterranean, DASH diet) emphasizing vegetables, fruits, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats, while limiting saturated fats, trans fats, dietary cholesterol, and refined carbohydrates.
Engage in at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity or 75 minutes per week of vigorous-intensity aerobic physical activity.
Maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 18.5-24.9 kg/m²) and reduce waist circumference.
Avoid tobacco products; for current smokers, provide counseling and resources for smoking cessation.
Limit alcohol consumption to no more than 1 drink per day for women and 2 drinks per day for men.
Manage stress through relaxation techniques, mindfulness, or other coping strategies.
Ensure adequate sleep (7-9 hours per night) for overall cardiovascular health.

followUp.frequency: Initially 4-12 weeks after statin initiation or dose change, then every 6-12 months for stable patients.
metrics: Liver function tests (ALT) if clinically indicated or at baseline
Creatine Kinase (CK) if muscle symptoms develop or at baseline if high risk
Assessment of medication adherence and side effects
Review of lifestyle modifications and progress

warnings: Statins are contraindicated in active liver disease or unexplained persistent elevations of serum transaminases.
Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to potential fetal harm.
Risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis increases with higher statin doses and certain drug interactions (e.g., strong CYP3A4 inhibitors with simvastatin/lovastatin).
Monitor for new-onset diabetes, especially in patients with pre-existing risk factors, though the cardiovascular benefits of statins generally outweigh this small risk.
Patients experiencing muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness should have CK levels checked.
Concomitant use of statins with fibrates (especially gemfibrozil) or niacin can increase the risk of myopathy.
Patients with severe primary hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) require high-intensity statin therapy regardless of ASCVD risk calculation.
Avoid grapefruit juice with simvastatin, lovastatin, and atorvastatin as it can increase statin levels and risk of side effects.
